title: Spring Boot — Why It Exists & How It Relates to Java EE

sections.heading: Spring Boot vs Java EE — same concepts, different names
sections.content:
If you know Java EE/Jakarta EE, many core concepts transfer directly to Spring Boot. The concepts are the same — the APIs and annotations differ.
ConceptJava EE / Jakarta EESpring Boot
DI ContainerCDI (Weld)Spring IoC (ApplicationContext)
Inject dependency@Inject@Autowired (or constructor)
Define bean@Stateless, @ApplicationScoped@Service, @Component
Transaction managementCMT (Container Managed)@Transactional
REST endpointsJAX-RS (@Path, @GET)Spring MVC (@RestController, @GetMapping)
PersistenceJPA + Hibernate (same!)Spring Data JPA (wraps same JPA)
MessagingJMS + MDB (@MessageDriven)Spring Kafka / @JmsListener
ConfigurationMicroProfile Configapplication.yml + @Value
App ServerWildFly, Payara, TomEEEmbedded Tomcat (no external server)
DeploymentWAR → app serverFat JAR (self-contained)
Key insight for interview: "I have 5+ years in Java EE with CDI, JPA, JMS. Spring Boot uses the same patterns with different annotations. My DI/transaction/messaging skills transfer directly — I just need to learn the Spring-specific API surface."

sections.heading: What Spring Boot adds over Spring Framework
Version context
sections.content: Reference versions for this content: • Spring Boot 3.x / 4.x (Jakarta EE 10+) • Java 17+ (minimum for Spring Boot 3) • Concepts valid across Spring Boot 3 and 4 Migration notes: • Spring Boot 3 moved to jakarta.* namespace (from javax.*) • Java 21 is the recommended runtime for new projects • Spring Boot 4.x requires Java 21+

sections.heading: CDI (@Inject) vs Spring (Constructor Injection)
sections.content:
Java EE (CDI) — field injection standard: 
@Stateless
public class DeliveryBusiness {
    @Inject private DeliveryRepository repository;
    @Inject private EventPublisher events;

    public Delivery plan(DeliveryRequest req) {
        Delivery d = Delivery.from(req);
        repository.persist(d);
        events.publish(new DeliveryPlanned(d));
        return d;
    }
}
Spring Boot — constructor injection preferred: 
@Service
public class DeliveryService {

    private final DeliveryRepository repository;
    private final LogisticsEventPublisher events;

    // Constructor injection — immutable, testable, no reflection
    public DeliveryService(DeliveryRepository repository,
                           LogisticsEventPublisher events) {
        this.repository = repository;
        this.events = events;
    }

    public Delivery plan(DeliveryRequest req) {
        Delivery d = Delivery.from(req);
        repository.save(d);
        events.publishDeliveryPlanned(d);
        return d;
    }
}
Why constructor injection is generally preferred: • Fields are final → immutable after construction → thread-safe • No reflection magic — plain Java constructor • Fails FAST at startup if dependency missing (not at runtime NPE) • Easy to test: new DeliveryService(mockRepo, mockEvents) • If constructor gets too big → signal to split the class (SRP feedback)

sections.heading: Bean Scopes — CDI vs Spring
sections.content:
PurposeCDI ScopeSpring Scope
One instance per app@ApplicationScoped@Scope("singleton") — DEFAULT
New per HTTP request@RequestScoped@RequestScope
Per HTTP session@SessionScoped@SessionScope
New per injection@Dependent@Scope("prototype")
EJB pooled@StatelessNo equivalent (singleton is enough)
JSF view lifetime@ViewScopedNo equivalent (use session or cache)
Key difference: Spring default is SINGLETON (one instance shared). CDI default depends on the annotation used. In Spring, most stateless service beans are singletons — other scopes are used for request-specific or session-specific state. Interview answer: "In Java EE, @Stateless gives you pooling and CMT. In Spring, @Service is singleton by default with @Transactional for transaction boundaries. The business outcome is similar, though the runtime model differs."

sections.heading: Spring stereotypes
sections.content:
@Component    // generic Spring-managed bean
@Service      // business logic (same as @Component semantically)
@Repository   // data access (adds exception translation)
@Controller   // web controller (returns views)
@RestController  // REST endpoints (= @Controller + @ResponseBody)
@Configuration   // defines @Bean factory methods
All are @Component under the hood. All are @Component specializations. @Repository additionally participates in persistence exception translation. The others mainly communicate architectural intent, although custom AOP pointcuts can target them. 
// Configuration class = replaces XML wiring
@Configuration
public class KafkaConfig {

    @Bean  // explicitly creates and configures a bean
    public KafkaTemplate<String, LogisticsEvent> kafkaTemplate(
            ProducerFactory<String, LogisticsEvent> factory) {
        return new KafkaTemplate<>(factory);
    }
}

sections.heading: From EntityManager to Spring Data
sections.content:
Java EE — manual repository: 
@Stateless
public class DeliveryRepository {
    @PersistenceContext
    private EntityManager em;

    public Delivery findById(Long id) {
        return em.find(Delivery.class, id);
    }

    public List<Delivery> findByStatus(DeliveryStatus status) {
        return em.createQuery(
            "SELECT d FROM Delivery d WHERE d.status = :status", Delivery.class)
            .setParameter("status", status)
            .getResultList();
    }

    public void save(Delivery d) { em.persist(d); }
}
Spring Data JPA — interface only: 
public interface DeliveryRepository extends JpaRepository<Delivery, Long> {

    // Method name → query (auto-generated!)
    List<Delivery> findByStatus(DeliveryStatus status);

    List<Delivery> findByDriverIdAndStatusIn(Long driverId, List<DeliveryStatus> statuses);

    @Query("SELECT d FROM Delivery d WHERE d.route.id = :routeId AND d.status = 'PLANNED'")
    List<Delivery> findPlannedForRoute(@Param("routeId") Long routeId);

    // save(), findById(), deleteById(), findAll() — all inherited!
}
How it works: Spring creates the implementation at runtime by parsing method names. findByStatusAndDriverId → WHERE status = ? AND driverId = ?. Interview tip: "Spring Data eliminates boilerplate repositories. For complex queries, I still use @Query with JPQL or native SQL. The same JPA/Hibernate I've used for years runs underneath."

sections.heading: Transactions — CMT vs @Transactional
sections.content:
Java EE — Container Managed Transactions (CMT): 
@Stateless  // Every public method is transactional by default!
public class DeliveryBusiness {
    // Transaction starts when method is called
    // Commits on success, rolls back on RuntimeException
    public Delivery plan(DeliveryRequest req) { ... }
}
Spring — explicit @Transactional: 
@Service
public class DeliveryService {

    @Transactional  // must declare explicitly
    public Delivery plan(DeliveryRequest req) {
        Delivery d = Delivery.from(req);
        repository.save(d);
        events.publishDeliveryPlanned(d);
        return d;
    }

    @Transactional(readOnly = true)  // expresses read-only intent
    public Delivery findById(Long id) {
        return repository.findById(id)
            .orElseThrow(() -> new DeliveryNotFoundException(id));
    }
}
Key differences: • Java EE: implicit (every EJB method is transactional by default) • Spring: explicit (must add @Transactional where needed) • Both: rollback on unchecked exceptions by default • Spring extra: readOnly = true expresses intent and may enable optimizations (e.g., skip dirty-checking) depending on the JPA provider • Spring extra: propagation controls nested TX behavior Both frameworks use container interception around managed invocations, but their runtime models differ. In Spring's default proxy mode, self-invocation (calling another method via this) bypasses transactional interception — the same conceptual issue exists in EJB when calling within the same bean instance.

title: REST Controllers — JAX-RS vs Spring MVC

sections.heading: Endpoint comparison
sections.content:
JAX-RS (Java EE): 
@Path("/deliveries")
@Produces(MediaType.APPLICATION_JSON)
@Consumes(MediaType.APPLICATION_JSON)
public class DeliveryEndpoint {

    @Inject private DeliveryService service;

    @GET
    @Path("/{id}")
    public Response findById(@PathParam("id") Long id) {
        return Response.ok(service.findById(id)).build();
    }

    @POST
    public Response create(@Valid DeliveryRequest req) {
        Delivery d = service.plan(req);
        URI location = UriBuilder.fromResource(DeliveryEndpoint.class)
            .path(d.getId().toString()).build();
        return Response.created(location).entity(d).build();
    }
}
Spring MVC: 
@RestController
@RequestMapping("/api/deliveries")
public class DeliveryController {

    private final DeliveryService service;

    @GetMapping("/{id}")
    public ResponseEntity<DeliveryDTO> findById(@PathVariable Long id) {
        return ResponseEntity.ok(service.findById(id));
    }

    @PostMapping
    public ResponseEntity<DeliveryDTO> create(@Valid @RequestBody DeliveryRequest req) {
        Delivery d = service.plan(req);
        URI location = ServletUriComponentsBuilder.fromCurrentRequest()
            .path("/{id}").buildAndExpand(d.getId()).toUri();
        return ResponseEntity.created(location).body(toDTO(d));
    }

    @ExceptionHandler(DeliveryNotFoundException.class)
    public ResponseEntity<ErrorResponse> handleNotFound(DeliveryNotFoundException e) {
        return ResponseEntity.status(404).body(new ErrorResponse(e.getMessage()));
    }
}
Mapping: @Path → @RequestMapping / @GetMapping @PathParam → @PathVariable @QueryParam → @RequestParam Response.ok() → ResponseEntity.ok() ExceptionMapper → @ExceptionHandler or @ControllerAdvice

sections.heading: Validation, Error handling, DTOs
application.yml and @ConfigurationProperties
sections.content:
Java EE — MicroProfile Config: 
// microprofile-config.properties
delivery.max.retries=3
delivery.timeout.ms=5000

@Inject @ConfigProperty(name = "delivery.max.retries")
private int maxRetries;
Spring Boot — application.yml + type-safe config: 
# application.yml
delivery:
  max-retries: 3
  timeout-ms: 5000
  kafka:
    topic: delivery-events
    consumer-group: trab-delivery-service
// Type-safe configuration (preferred over @Value)
@ConfigurationProperties(prefix = "delivery")
public record DeliveryProperties(
    int maxRetries,
    int timeoutMs,
    KafkaProperties kafka
) {
    public record KafkaProperties(String topic, String consumerGroup) {}
}
Advantages over MicroProfile Config: • Type-safe binding (not just strings) • Nested objects, lists, maps • Validation with @Validated • IDE auto-completion • Clear documentation via metadata

sections.heading: Profiles — environment-specific config
sections.content:
# application.yml (base — always loaded)
spring:
  application:
    name: delivery-service

# application-dev.yml (loaded when profile=dev)
spring:
  datasource:
    url: jdbc:postgresql://localhost:5432/delivery
  kafka:
    bootstrap-servers: localhost:9092

# application-prod.yml (loaded when profile=prod)
spring:
  datasource:
    url: jdbc:postgresql://prod-cluster:5432/delivery
  kafka:
    bootstrap-servers: kafka-prod:9092
Activation: 
# Environment variable (typical in Kubernetes)
SPRING_PROFILES_ACTIVE=prod

# JVM argument
java -jar app.jar --spring.profiles.active=prod

# Programmatic
@Profile("dev")
@Configuration
public class DevOnlyConfig { ... }
Java EE equivalent: System properties + ConfigSource priority (less structured). REWE scenario: Profile per environment (dev, staging, prod) + per region if needed. Kafka topics, DB URLs, feature flags all vary by profile.

sections.heading: Test slices and @MockBean
Interview answer on testing strategy
sections.content: "How do you test in Spring Boot?" "I follow the testing pyramid. Most tests are unit tests with Mockito — no Spring context, fast feedback. I test business logic in isolation. For repository queries, I use @DataJpaTest with Testcontainers (real PostgreSQL, not H2) to verify query correctness. For controllers, I use @WebMvcTest with MockMvc — tests serialization, validation, and error handling without starting the full app. Integration tests with @SpringBootTest + Testcontainers validate the full flow end-to-end, but I keep these minimal since they're slow. This is similar to how I tested in Java EE with JUnit + Mockito for business, plus Arquillian for integration — same pyramid, different tools."

title: Actuator, Health Checks, and Production Readiness

sections.heading: Spring Boot Actuator
Senior Perspective
sections.content:
Senior engineers don't just know how to configure Spring Boot — they understand the proxy model, transaction boundaries, and the cost of auto-configuration magic.

Key distinctions:
• Understanding self-invocation and why @Transactional fails on internal calls
• Knowing when @Async creates more problems than it solves
• Recognizing that 'convention over configuration' means you must understand the conventions
• Evaluating startup time, memory footprint and cold-start implications for serverless

sections.heading: REWE Perspective
sections.content:
In TRAB's logistics platform, Spring Boot services handle route planning, delivery tracking and warehouse communication.

Consider:
• How would you structure a service that processes 20K delivery events/hour?
• When would Spring WebFlux provide benefit over Spring MVC for this workload?
• How would you handle graceful shutdown when a Kafka consumer is mid-batch?
• What monitoring (Actuator, Micrometer) is essential for a logistics service?

sections.heading: When NOT to use
sections.content:
When NOT to use Spring Boot:

• Serverless functions where cold start matters (consider Quarkus/Micronaut with GraalVM)
• Ultra-low-latency systems where GC pauses or reflection overhead is unacceptable
• Simple CLI tools where a DI framework adds unnecessary complexity
• When the team needs fine-grained control over every dependency and configuration
• Kotlin-first projects where Ktor provides a more idiomatic experience

title: Spring Data JPA — Production Thinking Without Pretending Experience

sections.heading: What transfers directly from Java EE
sections.content: You do not need Spring production experience to reason well about persistence risks. The underlying ORM concerns are the same ones you already know from JPA/Hibernate: transaction boundaries, lazy loading, query volume, indexes, pagination, locking and data consistency. Spring Data removes repository boilerplate; it does not remove those responsibilities. Safe interview framing: "I have used JPA/Hibernate in production in Java EE. I have studied how Spring Data JPA wraps the same persistence model. I would inspect generated SQL, define transaction boundaries in the service layer, and validate the framework-specific configuration with the team before changing a production path."

sections.heading: A practical investigation checklist
sections.content: When an endpoint is slow, do not begin by changing annotations. Identify the request, inspect its SQL and measure the query count. An N+1 issue usually means a list query loads entities and then triggers extra queries while serializing or mapping associations. Questions to ask: • Is the response returning more fields than the caller needs? Use a DTO or projection. • Is an association really needed for this screen? Choose a fetch strategy deliberately. • Does a fetch join still work with pagination? A collection fetch join can distort page results. • Is the database index aligned with the filter and sort order? Do not say you tuned Spring Data JPA in production. Say that you know this diagnostic process from production JPA work and would apply it with Spring-specific observability and review.

sections.heading: Concurrency and consistency
sections.content: Optimistic locking is a design option when concurrent updates must not silently overwrite each other. An entity carries a version field; an update succeeds only if the version still matches. The important interview point is not the annotation: use a lock only when concurrent updates can violate a business invariant, define how the caller handles conflict, and test that path. This reasoning transfers directly from enterprise Java experience.

title: Spring MVC — Safe API Reasoning and an Honest Interview Answer

sections.heading: The framework changes; the API contract does not
sections.content: A Spring controller should remain thin: parse HTTP input, validate it, call a use case, and translate the result into an HTTP response. That is the same separation you already know from JAX-RS. Business rules belong in the service or domain layer; persistence stays behind a repository boundary. Safe wording: "My production REST experience is with JAX-RS. In Spring MVC the annotations and test tools differ, but I would keep the same API discipline: DTOs at the boundary, validation at input, consistent errors, and business logic outside the controller."

sections.heading: Error handling and security questions
sections.content: A useful error response lets a client act without exposing stack traces or internal implementation. Decide the error code, message, field violations and correlation identifier together, then centralize the mapping in @RestControllerAdvice. This is the Spring equivalent of the centralized exception translation pattern you already know. Before proposing Spring Security configuration, clarify the identity provider, token format, authorization model, public endpoints, service-to-service calls and audit requirements. A safe answer is: "I understand the common Spring Security model, but I would validate the organisation's identity and gateway standards before configuring it for production."

title: Configuration & Delivery — What You Can Explain Honestly

sections.heading: Configuration is input, not source code
sections.content: The principle transfers from Java EE and MicroProfile Config: environment-specific values must be externalized, typed where possible, validated on startup, and never committed as secrets. In Spring Boot, @ConfigurationProperties groups related settings into a typed object; profiles select environment-specific configuration. Important distinction: a profile is not a secret-management solution. Passwords, tokens and certificates should come from the organisation's approved secret store or runtime environment, not from a committed application-prod.yml file.

sections.heading: A safe production conversation
sections.content: You can discuss a deployment checklist without presenting it as Spring production history: confirm which profile and configuration source are active; validate required values at startup; ensure logs never print credentials; make a configuration change observable and reversible; and confirm ownership of database, messaging and external-service settings with the platform team. Interview wording: "I have handled externalized configuration in enterprise Java. For Spring Boot, I have studied profiles and typed configuration properties. I would follow the team's deployment and secret-management standards rather than inventing a configuration scheme alone."

title: Actuator & Operations — Fluent, Precise, and Honest

sections.heading: What you can safely demonstrate
sections.content: You can explain why health, metrics and logs matter without saying that you operated Spring Boot in production. Actuator exposes framework hooks for these concerns; the operational decision is still human: decide what proves the service is alive, what proves it can receive traffic, and which failures should alert a team. Transferable production experience: latency diagnosis, log correlation, query analysis, queue monitoring and incident investigation are operational skills. The Spring-specific piece to learn is how Actuator and Micrometer expose signals to the platform.

sections.heading: Readiness is not the same as liveness
sections.content:
A live process is not always ready to serve traffic. For example, an application may have started but still be unable to use a required database or message broker. A readiness signal protects callers from that state; a liveness signal helps the platform decide whether a process is stuck and needs restarting.

Do not turn every dependency hiccup into a restart loop. The correct health policy depends on whether the service can degrade safely, whether a dependency is essential for each request, and how the platform handles retries. Those are the questions to bring to a design review.

sections.heading: Interview answer: studied Spring, real operational foundations
sections.content: Use this answer: "I have not operated Spring Boot services in production yet. My production experience is with enterprise Java systems, where I investigated latency, logs, database behaviour and asynchronous processing. I understand that in Spring Boot, Actuator and metrics provide the hooks for health and observability. I would first learn the team's dashboards, alert rules and probe policy, then use the same diagnostic discipline I already apply in production." This is stronger than claiming familiarity you cannot defend: it names the gap, connects it to real evidence, and shows how you would become effective responsibly.

sections.heading: How @Transactional works (proxy-based AOP)
The self-invocation trap
sections.content:
The #1 Spring transaction pitfall: calling a @Transactional method from within the same class bypasses the proxy. 
@Service
public class OrderService {

    public void processOrder(Order order) {
        // ❌ This calls the METHOD DIRECTLY, not through the proxy!
        // @Transactional is IGNORED
        this.saveOrder(order);
    }

    @Transactional
    public void saveOrder(Order order) {
        repository.save(order);
        // No transaction! Changes may not be committed.
    }
}

// ✅ Fix: inject self or extract to another service
@Service
public class OrderService {
    @Autowired private OrderPersistence persistence;

    public void processOrder(Order order) {
        persistence.saveOrder(order); // Goes through proxy ✅
    }
}
Why? this.method() bypasses the proxy. Only calls from OUTSIDE the class go through the transactional proxy.

sections.heading: Propagation levels
sections.content:
PropagationBehaviorWhen to use
REQUIRED (default)Join existing TX or create newMost service methods
REQUIRES_NEWAlways create new TX (suspend current)Audit logs that must persist even on rollback
MANDATORYMust run within existing TX (throws if none)Repository methods that should never be called alone
NOT_SUPPORTEDSuspend TX, run withoutRead-only queries, external calls
NEVERThrow if TX existsMethods that must NOT run in transaction
@Transactional(propagation = Propagation.REQUIRES_NEW)
public void auditAction(String action, Long userId) {
    // This commits even if the caller rolls back
    auditRepository.save(new AuditEntry(action, userId, Instant.now()));
}

sections.heading: Rollback rules
sections.content:
Default behavior: • RuntimeException (unchecked) → rollback • Checked Exception → NO rollback (commit!) This surprises many developers: 
@Transactional
public void transfer(Long from, Long to, BigDecimal amount) throws InsufficientFundsException {
    debit(from, amount);
    credit(to, amount);
    // If InsufficientFundsException (checked) is thrown:
    // Transaction COMMITS! Partial state persisted! 💥
}

// ✅ Fix: explicit rollback rule
@Transactional(rollbackFor = Exception.class)
public void transfer(Long from, Long to, BigDecimal amount) throws InsufficientFundsException {
    // Now ANY exception triggers rollback
}
Best practice: Always use rollbackFor = Exception.class unless you have a specific reason not to.

sections.heading: Read-only optimization
sections.content:
@Transactional(readOnly = true)
public List<Delivery> findActiveDeliveries() {
    return repository.findByStatus(ACTIVE);
}

// What readOnly = true does:
// 1. Hints to DB driver (some DBs optimize read paths)
// 2. Hibernate skips dirty checking (no flush needed)
// 3. Connection may use read replica (if configured)
// 4. Documents intent — this method does NOT write
Rule of thumb: • Query methods → @Transactional(readOnly = true) • Write methods → @Transactional (or @Transactional(rollbackFor = Exception.class)) • Don't put @Transactional on Controllers — keep it at Service layer
